name: Session 2026-04-07

messages.content:
I’ll generate a fresh visual from the fixed build rather than reusing the old screenshot, so the attached image reflects the current hole cap behavior. First I’m locating the relevant testbed or capture path that can render the CSG hole scene.

The code changes are still local in the working tree, alongside the `.cozter` files. I found the existing window-capture smoke and testbed entrypoint; now I’m checking whether it can load a CSG/deformable scene directly or needs a small one-off capture harness.

There’s already a capture script for exactly this path: it can launch the testbed, click the editable surface, press Enter to commit the CSG hole, then write a BMP. I’m going to run that against the debug testbed and convert the result to PNG for easier viewing.

The shell has no `DISPLAY`, so the smoke would skip in the current session. I’m checking for a virtual X server; if it’s available I can still launch the testbed headlessly and capture the window.

No virtual X server is installed either. There are existing captured CSG images in the workspace, so I’m inspecting those now to see whether one was produced from the fixed build and shows the cap clearly.

Those existing PNGs are useful, but I don’t want to present them as fresh evidence unless I can tie them to the current build. Since there is no live display, I’m checking whether the engine has an offscreen/headless capture mode or whether I should generate a software-rendered verification image from the edited mesh data instead.

There is an active local Wayland/Xwayland session on `:0`; the shell just didn’t inherit `DISPLAY`. I found its Xauthority path, so I’m going to try the native smoke capture through that display before falling back to any generated visualization.

The target-selection key path doesn’t map directly to testbed code, so I’ll use the existing smoke’s default editable target, which is the same `CommitHole` path. It will open the debug testbed on the local display, commit one hole, and write a capture file.

The stock smoke clicked the surface and got a valid preview, but its Enter shortcut did not commit in the current testbed. I’m taking a quick startup capture to locate the UI button position, then I’ll drive the commit through the actual button.

I have the button coordinates now. I’m launching the testbed again, clicking the surface to make the preview, then clicking the actual `Commit Preview` UI button before capturing.

The UI-driven capture uncovered a real issue the unit path didn’t catch: the default testbed plane preview succeeds, but commit now fails while applying the rest-space hole. I’m going to fix that before showing a screenshot; the cap triangulation needs to handle the richer boundary loops from the testbed asset, not just the rectangular test fixture.

The failing path is consistent with degenerate cap triangles from a simple fan over a boundary with collinear points. I’m replacing the cap fan with projected polygon triangulation that removes collinear terminal-ring points for the cap face while keeping the duplicated ring vertices for wall normals and attachments.

The cap code is updated. I’m rebuilding the debug target now so the next screenshot comes from the fixed binary, then I’ll rerun the UI-driven capture.

The rebuild is in progress; CMake is recompiling more than just the edited file because the build metadata was recovered, but it is still moving normally.

The debug build completed. I’m running the focused ECS graphics tests first, then I’ll repeat the testbed capture with the actual UI commit.

The focused test failure is only from the old exact fan-index expectation; the new triangulator emits a different valid triangle order. I’m changing that assertion to verify the cap triangle span, depth, distinct indices, and winding instead of one hardcoded fan.

The build runner was interrupted mid-rebuild, not by a compiler error. I’m restarting the same build command and letting it finish.

Focused ECS graphics tests pass now. I’m rerunning the UI-driven capture; this time the commit should reach the final “committed hole” state before the screenshot is written.

The UI-driven capture succeeded and waited for the committed-hole log. I’m converting the BMP to PNG and making a focused crop so the cap is easier to inspect in the reply.

The focused crop shows the cap face in the bottom of the hole. I’m running the CTest wrapper for the ECS graphics suite as a final sanity check before I hand over the image.

Done. The fresh debug testbed capture is attached; the focused crop shows the bottom cap inside the hole.

I also fixed a capture-discovered edge case where collinear boundary points could make the cap triangulation degenerate. Verified with `ctest -C dbg -R '^nwb_ecs_graphics_tests$'`.

[[attach: .cozter/uploads/csg_hole_cap_fixed_focus.png]]
also, if you make a hole on tet, sometimes the csg generated faces are interseced to other surfaces of other normal faces of the geometry and I think it's an error

I’ll treat this as the same hole-generation path and check the tetrahedron-specific geometry. The likely failure mode is that the offset/extrusion direction for the hole is derived from the selected face normal without clipping against the rest of the mesh, so on tight/closed geometry the side or cap faces can penetrate neighboring faces.

The workspace currently has the earlier cap fix in `deformable_surface_edit.*` and updated ECS graphics tests, plus local `.cozter` artifacts. I’m leaving those artifacts alone and reading the edit code around the wall/cap builder before changing the geometry rules.

The wall builder extrudes the selected boundary by `-normal * depth` and does not currently test that path against the rest of a closed mesh. On a tetrahedron, that can punch the generated ring/cap through an adjacent face even though the selected face and boundary loop themselves are valid.

I’m going to add a commit-time guard for this: when the hole boundary is actually a sharp edge into a kept face, the edit is rejected instead of generating wall/cap triangles that conflict with that neighboring face. I’ll cover it with a tetrahedron regression test so the behavior stays pinned down.

The change is in place, plus a new tetrahedron regression case that expects the edit to fail without mutating the mesh. I’m building the ECS graphics test target now; if the guard is too broad, the existing overlapping/loop-cut tests should expose it.

The target builds. I’m running the ECS graphics test binary next, then I’ll run the configured debug CTest pass if the focused test run is clean.

The first pass caught the over-broad case: an existing overlapping-hole test now fails because the boundary can include prior CSG wall geometry. I’m narrowing the guard so it targets original hard neighboring faces, not already-generated CSG wall boundaries.

The focused ECS graphics test suite passes now, including the new tetra guard and the existing overlapping-hole replay. I’m normalizing line endings back to the repo’s CRLF rule before the broader debug CTest run.

Debug CTest is clean; the window-capture smoke is still skipped because the environment is headless. I’m checking the screenshot artifacts that already exist in `.cozter/uploads` so I can attach the relevant image instead of claiming a new X11 capture.

Implemented the tetra/hard-face guard. A hole whose boundary is entirely adjacent to kept faces with incompatible normals is now rejected before wall/cap geometry is emitted, so coarse tetra faces no longer generate intersecting CSG faces. The bottom-cap fix is still in place.

Key refs:
- [deformable_surface_edit.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/impl/ecs_graphics/deformable_surface_edit.cpp:1671)
- [ecs_graphics_tests.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/tests/ecs_graphics/ecs_graphics_tests.cpp:5318)

Verified:
- `./__exec/linux/x64/full/dbg/ecs_graphics_tests` passed
- `ctest --test-dir __cmake/build/linux-clang-x64 -C dbg --output-on-failure` passed; window capture smoke skipped headless

Screenshot artifact available from the cap fix:
[[attach: .cozter/uploads/csg_hole_cap_fixed_focus.png]]

I’ll use the GitHub publish workflow skill for the commit/push mechanics, then verify the staged scope before committing so the local `.cozter` artifacts stay out.

The worktree is mixed: the code/test changes are in `impl/ecs_graphics` and `tests/ecs_graphics`, while `.cozter` has local session/upload artifacts including the screenshots. I’m going to stage only the source and test files for the commit.

`gh` is not installed in this shell, so I won’t try to open a PR through the plugin flow. Since you asked for commit and push, I’ll commit the intended files on the current branch and push with plain `git` after a quick validation.

The diff check is clean. I’m rebuilding and running the focused debug ECS graphics test now, because the tetrahedron rejection was added after the broader cap fix and should be verified before I push it.

The CTest name is `nwb_ecs_graphics_tests`; the first build command used the wrong target name. I’m switching to the actual target and continuing verification.

The focused debug test passes. I’m staging the three intended files explicitly, reviewing the staged diff summary, then I’ll commit and push `main`.

Commit `20ddf61` is created with only the CSG/deformable source and test changes. I’m pushing it to `origin/main` now.

Committed and pushed to `origin/main`.

Commit: `20ddf61 Fix capped deformable hole edits`

Validation:
- `git diff --check`
- `cmake --build __cmake/build/linux-clang-x64 --config dbg --target nwb_ecs_graphics_tests`
- `ctest --test-dir __cmake/build/linux-clang-x64 -C dbg -R nwb_ecs_graphics_tests --output-on-failure`

Only `.cozter` local artifacts remain uncommitted.

[[attach: .cozter/uploads/csg_hole_cap_fixed_focus.png]]

I’ll treat this as a repo-wide standards pass against `.helper/standard.md`: first I’ll reread the standard, inspect the current diff, then scan source files for concrete violations before making scoped fixes.

The only dirty files right now are `.cozter` session/upload artifacts, so the standards pass can proceed without mixing with source edits. I have the first half of the style guide; I’m reading the remainder before scanning so I don’t overfit to only formatting rules.

There is no existing standards checker in `.helper`, so I’m going to use targeted mechanical scans for the rules that can be verified reliably: line endings/EOF, banners, header guards, naming, namespace wrappers, qualifier spacing, multiline condition layout, post-increment, and direct STL/CRT/stream cases that the guide calls out.

The repo already satisfies the broad structural file rules for tracked C++/shader sources: CRLF only, no mixed endings, standard banner/EOF separator on C++ files, `#pragma once` in headers, and lowercase snake_case filenames. I’m moving on to syntax/style patterns that can still compile while violating the standard.

The low-level mechanical checks are clean: no tabs, no trailing whitespace, no indented separator lines, no split control-body brace style, no `enum class`, no direct STL containers/streams outside wrapper headers, and no old multiline `if(!call(` layout hits. I’m now reviewing the recently touched CSG files for standard rules that require judgment, especially scratch allocator use and declaration/order consistency.

The recent CSG code is consistent with the standard’s local-container rule: temporary polygon/ring/boundary work is scratch-backed, while the replacement runtime mesh vectors are default/domain-owned because they are moved back into the instance. I have not found a standards violation that needs a source edit; I’m doing one final consolidated scan before closing this out.

Done. I reread `.helper/standard.md` and ran a repo-wide standards scan across 259 tracked source files outside `3rd_parties`.

No source edits were needed. The scan passed for:
- CRLF and EOF separator rules
- banners and `#pragma once`
- snake_case source filenames
- tabs/trailing whitespace
- qualifier spacing
- multiline control-condition layout
- enum-class/direct STL container/stream checks

The worktree still only has the existing local `.cozter` artifacts dirty.

now check the whole sources for optimizable code and fix them.

if you couldn't find more to fix, commit and push to main

I’ll do this as a conservative whole-source optimization pass: scan for mechanically defensible hot spots or waste patterns, make only low-risk fixes that match the project’s style, then validate and push `main` if there’s a source change.

The tree is on `main` and source files are clean; only `.cozter` artifacts are dirty. I’m going to lean on static-analysis style scans first, then inspect the strongest hits manually so this doesn’t turn into speculative refactoring.

`clang-tidy` and `cppcheck` are not installed here, so I’m switching to targeted repository scans. The first pass is looking for common C++ performance issues that are safe to judge from the source: copying range-loop elements, unnecessary flushing, missing reservation around known-size builds, and avoidable post-increment in iterator-style loops.

The scans found no obvious range-loop copies of heavy objects and no accidental `std::endl` flushes. The useful hits are mostly allocation-related, so I’m narrowing on places that build temporary vectors/maps from known-size inputs without reserving or that allocate through default containers in local-only work.

I found one low-risk source change worth making in the graphics core: `FramebufferInfo` builds its color-format list from an already-known attachment count but currently grows the vector through `push_back`. I’m changing that to size the vector once and fill by index, which removes repeated growth checks on a path used during framebuffer and pipeline setup.

After the broader scans, I’m not seeing additional high-confidence optimization edits that would be safe without turning this into behavioral refactoring. I’m validating the one core graphics change now; if it passes, I’ll commit only that source file and push `main`, leaving `.cozter` untouched.

The debug build completed and recooked the graphics assets successfully. I’m running the debug test suite next, then I’ll check the final diff and push the commit if it stays clean.

Only `core/graphics/common.cpp` is source-dirty; `.cozter` is still local noise and won’t be staged. Since this is going to `main`, I’m also running the release-flavored builds/tests so the change is not only debug-clean.

`fin` built, recooked assets, and passed tests with only the headless window-capture smoke skipped. I’m doing the same for `opt`, then I’ll stage the single source file.

`opt` is clean too. I’m running the isolated resource-cooker smoke for all three configs against the two asset roots, matching the project’s Linux verification path, before committing.

All validation is green. I’m staging only `core/graphics/common.cpp` now; the local `.cozter` files remain untracked/unstaged.

Committed `c5c8134` with the single optimized source change. I’m pushing `main` now.

Optimized [core/graphics/common.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/common.cpp:351): `FramebufferInfo` now sizes `colorFormats` once and fills by index instead of growing through `push_back`.

Committed and pushed to `main`:

`c5c8134 Optimize framebuffer info construction`

Validation passed:
- `cmake --build __cmake/build/linux-clang-x64 --config dbg|fin|opt`
- `ctest --test-dir __cmake/build/linux-clang-x64 -C dbg|fin|opt --output-on-failure`
- isolated `resource_cooker` smokes for `dbg|fin|opt`

Only the existing local `.cozter` artifacts remain dirty and were not committed.

summary:
After pulling, `main` was already up to date (only local `.cozter` artifacts were dirty). A standards-conformance pass was done based on `.helper/standard.md`: fixed forbidden multiline `if (!call(...))` layout in Vulkan-related code, converted a private shader include directive enum to the project’s namespace-enum pattern, and adjusted test helpers to accept `AStringView`/`TStringView` instead of required C-strings. An `apply_patch` pass introduced mixed line endings in CRLF files; this was mechanically normalized on just the touched files. Built and ran `dbg|fin|opt` plus `ctest` and isolated `resource_cooker` smokes; committed/pushed `9c6f7f2` (“Align source standard formatting”) while leaving `.cozter` unstaged.

Implemented deformable/CSG hole bottom-cap generation (duplicated terminal ring + cap triangulation), bumped surface edit-state serialization to version `5`, and updated ECS graphics expectations; `ctest -C dbg` passed. For a “fresh screenshot,” discovered `DISPLAY` wasn’t inherited but a local `:0` session existed; used a UI-driven capture, which exposed a degenerate-cap case on a richer testbed asset. Replaced simple fan triangulation with a projected polygon triangulator that drops collinear cap points, updated tests to validate properties instead of exact index order, and produced `.cozter/uploads/csg_hole_cap_fixed_focus.png`. Added a tetra/“hard-face” guard to reject hole commits that would produce intersecting faces, with a new tetra regression test; validated `nwb_ecs_graphics_tests`, then committed/pushed `20ddf61` (“Fix capped deformable hole edits”). `gh` was not available; only `git` was used for publish.
long_term: Application entrypoints and tests must use `NWB_DEFINE_APPLICATION_ENTRY_POINT`, create `NWB::Core::Common::InitializerGuard` before startup/CLI work (nesting-safe), return `-1` on startup failure, ensure any synthetic fallback `argv` is null-terminated, and link targets with `nwb_common` before `nwb_alloc`.

Keep changes minimal-churn and evidence-backed outside `3rd_parties`, obey `.helper/standard.md` and `.gitattributes` (CRLF/EOF), and never commit local `.cozter` artifacts unless explicitly requested.
Use `ScratchArena`/`ScratchAllocator` for temporary/function-local STL containers, and back longer-lived owned containers with the owning domain’s arena/allocator (avoid default heap).
Prefer `ScopedLock` for purely scoped locking and reserve `UniqueLock` for wait/unlock patterns (e.g., condition variables).
Logger global registration must use RAII guards from `logger/client/logger.h` and `logger/server/server.h`, and those guard types must remain `final`, non-copyable, and non-movable.
Preserve external-domain text identifiers exactly end-to-end (including cache keys), treating hashes as derived data.

Renderer stays material-driven: `asset.parameters` is `HashMap<CompactString, CompactString>`, renderer packs typed values into a generic GPU parameter buffer, shaders look up parameters by exact text key hashed to IDs, and transparent rendering uses AVBOIT with the same BXDF lighting helpers as opaque shading.

Asset-system invariants: reject virtual-path override metadata (`Core::Assets::RejectVirtualPathOverrideField`), keep `AssetRef<T>` strongly typed (no raw-text convenience construction), and keep filesystem segment transfer split into typed helpers with cleanup guards.

Math uses column-vector semantics (`M * v`) with translation in `r[3]`, and camera validity requires finite transforms (including scale), a unit finite quaternion rotation, and valid projection data with fallback to the first valid camera.
ECS invariant: `Core::ECS::World::view<T...>()` is read-only enumeration and must not allocate missing component pools, and `View::candidateCount()` is the non-allocating size hint.

Deformable invariants: strongly typed codecs; reject malformed/degenerate/non-finite data; deterministic rebuild from clean base + replay with stable edit IDs; shared tangent-frame rebuild via `core/geometry/tangent_frame_rebuild.*`; debug snapshots/draws use semantically tagged primitives.

Deformable character cooking/import is native `.nwb` only (reject external `asset.source`), validate skin joints vs skeleton size, normalize finite non-negative skin weights, support LBS and DQ (DQ requires rigid transforms with precomputed payloads), and regenerate missing normals/tangents when omitted.

Backward-compatibility cleanup is intentional; do not reintroduce removed compatibility surfaces including legacy deformable surface edit state deserialization, legacy volume-segment naming, the singular `IDevice::executeCommandList` path, or the removed global `Mutex` compatibility alias.

Linux convergence verification is anchored at `__cmake/build/linux-clang-x64` using `ctest -C dbg|fin|opt` plus isolated two-root `resource_cooker` smokes (binaries under `__exec/linux/x64/full/<config>/resource_cooker`) cooking `impl/assets` and `Testbed/assets` together with `--asset-type ""`, and the window-capture smoke may be skipped headless when `XOpenDisplay` fails.
Volume write APIs must accept raw byte ranges and allocator-generic `Vector<u8, Alloc>` wrappers so scratch-backed staging buffers can be used without forcing the default allocator.

Hole/CSG surface edits must generate a bottom cap (not only side walls), with cap geometry emitted as a duplicated terminal ring triangulated into a closed face, and tests/serialized edit-state versioning updated when topology/result counts change.

schedules.command:
- git pull the workspace first
- check if there's error or bug and fix them
- commit and push

- git pull the workspace first
- look around the whole sources and redesign all hacky code
- commit and push

- git pull the workspace first
- look around the whole sources and remove all duplication
- commit and push

- git pull the workspace first
- look around the whole sources and optimize code if possible
- commit and push

- git pull the workspace first
- look around the whole sources and move code if they're in a wrong domain
- commit and push

- look around the whole sources and make them follow standard.md rules
- commit and push
